core.intro: Wherever you are in Australia, limes want the same things: full sun, free-draining soil, steady water and feeding, and the warmest, most frost-free spot you can give them, because limes are the most cold-sensitive of the common citrus. The state sections cover the climate, growing regions, harvest timing and pests where you are, and (for WA) the citrus biosecurity rules. The notes below apply anywhere in the country.

sections.heading: Choosing a variety
sections.body: The Tahitian lime (also sold as Persian or Bearss lime) is the standard home lime: a large, green, seedless, thornless and juicy fruit, the main variety grown across coastal Australia, the hardiest of the true limes and with good tolerance of tristeza virus. The West Indian lime (also called Mexican or Key lime) is small, very tart and intensely aromatic, but it is thorny, more frost-tender and very prone to tristeza, so it suits only warm districts and is always best grafted. The makrut lime (still widely sold as kaffir lime) is grown mainly for its fragrant double leaves used in Thai and South-East Asian cooking, not for its knobbly, dry fruit. Rangpur lime and sweet lime are not true limes: Rangpur is a tart orange-coloured mandarin hybrid used in cooking and as a rootstock, and sweet lime is a mild, low-acid fruit. Australia also has its own native limes: the Desert Lime (Citrus glauca) from the arid inland is exceptionally drought and frost hardy, the Australian Round Lime (Citrus australis) is a subtropical rainforest species, and the CSIRO-bred Blood Lime (sold as Red Centre Lime) and Sunrise Lime are native hybrids grown for their unusual coloured, caviar-like flesh. The Australian finger lime is a separate species with its own page (see our finger lime guide).

sections.heading: Pollination: do you need two trees?
sections.body: No. Limes, like almost all citrus, are self-fertile, so a single tree sets a full crop on its own with no need for a second tree or a pollinator. The Tahitian lime goes one better and is parthenocarpic, meaning it sets fruit without fertilisation at all, which is why its fruit is seedless. So if you have room for only one citrus, a lime is an easy choice.

sections.heading: Planting and soil
sections.body: Give a lime full sun, at least six hours a day, and free-draining soil. A deep sandy loam is ideal; on heavy clay that drains slowly, plant on a low mound so water never ponds around the trunk, because limes are very prone to collar and root rot (Phytophthora) in wet feet. Keep the graft (bud union) well clear of the soil. Limes prefer a slightly acidic soil, about pH 6 to 6.5, where nutrients are most available. They have no frost hardiness to spare, so choose the warmest, most sheltered, frost-free corner of the garden, and shelter young trees from cold wind. Mulch well to hold moisture and feed the surface roots, but keep the mulch a hand-width back from the trunk so the bark stays dry. Container-grown trees can go in through most of the year as long as you keep the water up over summer (see our guide on when to plant).

sections.heading: Water and feeding
sections.body: Limes are thirsty and hungry trees that reward steady care. Water deeply and regularly through the warm months and never let a young or fruiting tree dry out: the make-or-break window runs from flowering through to fruit swell, and erratic or short watering at that stage shows up as dropped flowers, dropped fruitlets and split fruit. Mulch to even out soil moisture, but keep drainage sharp, because a few days of waterlogging invites root rot. On feeding, limes are heavy feeders and nitrogen is the key nutrient: it drives the strong spring growth flush that carries the next crop. Use a complete citrus fertiliser that is high in nitrogen (or organic equivalents such as well-rotted manure, blood and bone and compost), scaling the amount to the size and age of the tree and feeding little and often rather than in one hit: light feeds every couple of months while the tree is young, then a few generous feeds a year once it is bearing, in late winter, again after fruit set, and in late summer, easing off by mid autumn so the new growth hardens before winter. Watch for trace-element hunger, which is common on limes: a zinc foliar spray is often needed, and iron and magnesium lock up on alkaline soils (correct iron with chelates and magnesium with Epsom salts). Keeping the soil around pH 6 to 6.5 keeps all of these nutrients available.

sections.heading: Harvest: pick them green
sections.body: Limes flower in spring and the main crop follows through summer into winter, with the warmer (further north) you grow them spreading the picking across more of the year. The key is to pick limes while they are full sized but still green, because that is when the juice and aroma are at their best, and picking green also heads off stylar-end rot and the fruit drop that follows if they hang too long. Left on the tree a lime slowly pales and then turns yellow, going soft and losing its tang, so a yellow lime is over-ripe, not riper. Citrus do not keep ripening once picked, so harvest limes as you need them and use them fresh; they do not store for long.

sections.heading: Buying tips
sections.body: Buy a grafted, named tree on a named rootstock rather than a cheap seedling. Grafting puts your chosen variety onto a rootstock that supplies what the lime itself lacks: tolerance of tristeza virus, resistance to root and collar rot, and the right vigour and soil fit (trifoliata and the citranges suit cold or wet soils, while rough lemon suits lighter, drier ground). A grafted tree also crops in a couple of years where a seedling is slow, thorny and unpredictable. For a pot or a small courtyard, ask for a dwarf tree, usually grafted on Flying Dragon trifoliata, which stays around head height and fruits well. The in-stock table above shows the named varieties available right now and what they cost.

faqs.q: How much does a lime tree cost?
faqs.a: Most potted limes from the nurseries we track run from about $30 to $60, with dwarf grafted forms and larger advanced trees costing more, and big established or barrel-grown specimens running into the hundreds. The live table above lists the named limes in stock right now with their current prices, so you can compare before you buy.

faqs.q: How big does a lime tree get, and can I keep it in a pot?
faqs.a: A lime on a standard rootstock grows into a rounded tree around 3 to 5 metres, but it takes pruning well and is easily held smaller. For a balcony, courtyard or small garden, buy a dwarf grafted tree (usually on Flying Dragon trifoliata): it stays near head height, crops happily in a large pot, and can be moved into a warm spot in cold weather. Use a free-draining mix, water often because pots dry out fast, and feed regularly since nutrients wash out of containers.

faqs.q: How long until a lime tree starts fruiting?
faqs.a: A grafted, named lime usually carries its first useful crop two to three years after planting. A tree grown from a pip is far slower and may take six years or more, and the fruit can be variable, which is the main reason a grafted tree is worth paying for.

It depends on the type. The popular Tahitian lime is nearly thornless, which is one reason it is the easy backyard pick. The West Indian (Key) lime and Rangpur lime are thorny, and young citrus of any kind tend to throw more thorns than older wood, so wear gloves when you prune. The thorns do not affect fruit quality.

faqs.q: Can I grow a lime tree indoors?
faqs.a: A lime can spend cold months in a bright, sunny indoor spot or a glasshouse, which is how gardeners in cold districts keep tender limes alive, but it is not a true houseplant: it needs as much direct light as possible, good airflow and careful watering, and it will fruit far better if it summers outdoors in full sun. Grow it in a pot you can wheel in and out with the seasons.

WA.intro: Growing limes in Western Australia. Perth's warm, dry Mediterranean climate suits citrus well, and limes do fine here given the warmest, most frost-free corner of the garden. The thing that really sets a lime apart in WA is biosecurity: the state keeps citrus diseases out with strict import rules, so where you buy your tree matters as much as how you grow it.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: The Perth and south-west climate, with hot dry summers and mild wet winters, is good citrus country. Limes are the cold-sensitive exception, so they want a sheltered, sunny, frost-free position; in the frost-prone Perth Hills and the cold inland a young lime needs protection or a pot that can be moved. Further north the climate turns ideal: the subtropical Gascoyne and the tropical Ord are frost-free and crop limes for much of the year.

sections.heading: Where limes grow in WA
sections.body: Western Australia's citrus runs from the Mediterranean south-west, around Gingin, Bindoon, Chittering, the Perth Hills and Harvey, up to the warm north. The Gascoyne around Carnarvon is a frost-free, subtropical fruit district, and on the Ord River around Kununurra limes and grapefruit are grown commercially. Plenty of Perth backyards carry a lime as well.

sections.heading: Buying a lime in WA: the biosecurity rules
sections.body: Western Australia controls which live plants may enter the state through the WA Organism List and a Quarantine WA permit and conditions-of-entry system, and citrus is a high-biosecurity group, so many eastern-states nurseries simply will not post citrus trees across the border. The rules exist to keep out diseases such as citrus canker, which WA worked hard to eradicate and stay free of. The practical upshot for a WA gardener is to buy a WA-grown, certified lime from a local nursery rather than trying to bring one in from interstate. The in-stock table above is filtered to sellers that can ship to WA.

sections.heading: Pests in WA
sections.body: WA's main fruit pest is Mediterranean fruit fly (Medfly), which is established right across the state and treats citrus trees as a favoured overwintering refuge, so a backyard lime needs an ongoing Medfly program even when it is not carrying fruit. Queensland fruit fly is a prohibited pest that WA actively works to keep out, with an incursion being eradicated in Perth's southern suburbs in 2026. Citrus gall wasp has also become established across most Perth suburbs: cut out the woody galls in the new wood over winter, before the adult wasps emerge in spring.

faqs.q: Which lime grows best in Perth?
faqs.a: For most Perth gardens the Tahitian lime is the reliable pick: hardy, seedless and thornless, given a warm sunny spot. If your garden is hot, dry and low-water, the native Desert Lime is superbly tough. Keep the tender types, the West Indian (Key) lime and the makrut (kaffir) lime, in pots so you can shelter them from a cold snap in the hills or inland.

faqs.q: Can I order a lime tree from an interstate nursery to WA?
faqs.a: Usually not. Citrus is tightly controlled at the WA border to keep diseases like citrus canker out, so most eastern nurseries will not send citrus to WA at all, and bringing one in yourself can be illegal. Buy a certified, WA-grown lime from a local nursery instead; the table above only shows sellers that can supply to WA.

QLD.intro: Growing limes in Queensland. Queensland is the warm-climate heartland of the Australian lime: it grows more limes than any other state, and in the subtropics and tropics a Tahitian lime will carry fruit for much of the year.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Queensland's warm, frost-free, subtropical to tropical climate is as good as it gets for limes. Trees crop heavily and, in the north, almost continuously, and the heat even suits the tender West Indian (Key) lime that struggles further south. The trade-off is humidity: warm wet summers raise the pressure from fungal blemishes on the leaves and rind, so air flow and a sunny, open position help.

sections.heading: Where limes grow in QLD
sections.body: The Central Burnett around Gayndah and Mundubbera is Queensland's major citrus district, with more plantings around Bundaberg, and these warm inland and coastal districts grow limes alongside their mandarins and lemons. In the far north, the Atherton Tableland around Mareeba and Dimbulah is the single biggest lime-producing area in Australia, which is why Queensland leads the country for limes.

sections.heading: Harvest window in QLD
sections.body: Warm Queensland gives the longest lime season in the country: a heavy crop through summer and autumn and scattered fruit across much of the year in the tropical north. As everywhere, pick the fruit while it is still green and full sized rather than waiting for it to yellow.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in QLD
sections.body: Queensland fruit fly is endemic and stings citrus among its many hosts, so backyard limes need baiting, netting or prompt clean-up of fallen fruit. The warm, humid summers also favour fungal blemishes on the skin (such as melanose), which mark the rind without spoiling the flesh, and citrus gall wasp is native to this part of Australia. Queensland has had citrus canker outbreaks, in 1984 and again in 2004, but both were eradicated and Australia is now declared free of the disease.

faqs.q: Why does my Queensland lime have black specks or blotches on the skin?
faqs.a: In the humid Queensland summer that is almost always a cosmetic skin blemish, such as melanose or sooty mould growing on insect honeydew, not something rotting the fruit. The flesh inside is usually perfectly good. Improve air flow with a light prune, keep the tree well fed and watered, and pick the fruit green rather than letting it hang and weather.

faqs.q: Which lime should I plant in tropical north Queensland?
faqs.a: You can grow almost any lime. The Tahitian lime is the dependable, seedless all-rounder, but the north is one of the few places in Australia warm enough to do justice to the small, intensely tart West Indian (Key) lime, so it is worth a tree if you love that sharp, aromatic flavour for cooking and drinks.

NSW.intro: Growing limes in New South Wales. NSW takes in both the largest citrus region in Australia and the country's best subtropical lime country, so where you are in the state largely decides how easy a lime tree will be.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: New South Wales has two very different lime climates. The warm, frost-free, subtropical north coast is where limes grow most easily, with humid summers that the trees love. The inland Riverina is hot in summer but has cold, frosty winters, so a lime there needs the warmest, most sheltered spot and some frost protection while it is young. The cold tablelands are really too frosty for limes in the open ground, and are better suited to a movable pot.

sections.heading: Where limes grow in NSW
sections.body: The Riverina around Griffith and Leeton is the largest citrus region in Australia, but its plantings are mostly frost-hardy oranges and mandarins rather than tender limes. For limes, the natural home is the subtropical Northern Rivers, around Alstonville, Lismore and the Tweed, and the mid-north coast around Coffs Harbour, where the warm, humid, frost-free conditions suit them best.

sections.heading: Harvest window in NSW
sections.body: A Tahitian lime in NSW carries its main crop from autumn into winter, and the warm north coast spreads picking across more of the year than the cooler inland districts. Pick the limes while they are still green for the best flavour, rather than waiting for them to turn yellow.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in NSW
sections.body: Citrus gall wasp is the headline backyard citrus pest down the NSW coast. This native wasp, whose original host is the Australian finger lime, lays into the soft new wood and raises woody galls, and it has spread south through Sydney and beyond, so cut out and bin any galled wood in winter before the adults emerge in spring. Citrus leafminer silvers and curls the new leaves through the warm months, and Queensland fruit fly stings the fruit, especially on the coast and in the north.

faqs.q: Should I grow a different lime on the NSW coast than in the inland Riverina?
faqs.a: Yes, let the winters decide. On the warm, frost-free north coast you can grow any lime, including the tender West Indian (Key) lime. In the frosty inland Riverina, stick to the hardiest Tahitian lime in the warmest, most sheltered spot, or grow it in a pot you can move under cover when frost threatens.

faqs.q: Do lime trees drop their leaves in winter in NSW?
faqs.a: Citrus are evergreen, so a healthy lime should hold its leaves all year. Some leaf drop after cold snaps, dry spells or a heavy crop is normal, but a lime stripping bare usually means frost damage, waterlogging or root trouble. In the cold tablelands, frost can defoliate a young tree, which is why shelter and a warm position matter so much there.

VIC.intro: Growing limes in Victoria. Victoria sits at the cold edge of lime country: limes are the most cold-sensitive of the common citrus, so outside the warm Murray Valley most Victorians grow them in a pot that can be sheltered from frost.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Limes are among the most frost-tender citrus, which makes Victoria marginal for them. The warm, dry inland Murray Valley is the one part of the state reliably warm enough to grow a lime in the ground, and even there winter frost can damage young trees and fruit. Melbourne and the cooler, damper south are really too cold and grey for dependable cropping in the open garden, so the answer there is a pot and the hardiest variety you can find.

sections.heading: Where limes grow in VIC
sections.body: Victoria's citrus is concentrated in the warm Murray Valley (the Sunraysia district) around Mildura, Robinvale and Swan Hill, the state's main citrus-growing area. Those orchards are mostly oranges and mandarins, with limes the most cold-limited of the citrus grown there. South of the Great Dividing Range, limes are a courtyard-and-pot crop rather than a field one.

sections.heading: Growing limes in pots and cool spots
sections.body: The practical Victorian approach is a large pot you can move. Stand it in the sunniest, most sheltered position through the warm months, against a warm north-facing brick wall if you have one, and shift it under cover (a verandah, a bright shed, or indoors) before the first hard frosts. Use a free-draining citrus mix, water and feed regularly because pots dry and leach quickly, and start with a dwarf grafted Tahitian, the most cold-tolerant of the limes. Native Desert Lime, which shrugs off light frost, is another tough option.

sections.heading: Pests in VIC
sections.body: Queensland fruit fly has become established in the Murray Valley, and the Sunraysia region runs a coordinated fruit-fly control effort, so net or bait your fruit and never leave fallen limes lying on the ground. Cooler southern Victoria has far less fruit-fly pressure. Citrus gall wasp has also spread into Victoria, so prune out any woody galls in winter.

faqs.q: Which lime is the most cold hardy for Victoria?
faqs.a: Among the true limes the Tahitian lime is clearly the hardiest, so it is the one to start with, ideally as a dwarf grafted tree in a pot. The native Desert Lime is tougher still and tolerates light frost. Avoid the tender West Indian (Key) and makrut (kaffir) limes unless you can keep them potted and move them under cover for winter.

faqs.q: Do lime trees need a cold winter to fruit?
faqs.a: No, and this is where limes differ from apples, pears and stone fruit. Citrus need no winter chilling to crop, so a cold Victorian winter does nothing to help a lime fruit and only risks frost damage. Warmth, sun and steady water and feeding, not cold, are what bring on a lime crop.
